@prha brought up that fetching all the asset events could be expensive in some situations. E.g. imagine an AND sensor that depends on two assets: an asset that gets updated every minute and an asset that gets updated every month. By the end of the month, every tick would be pulling out 24 * 30 * 60 materializations from the DB.

A way to avoid this issue would be to create a MultiAssetSensorContext and replace the "asset_events" argument with methods on the context. Different methods could return the latest materialization or all materializations, depending on what the user cares about.  Thoughts?

Lastly, a few cosmetic things to think about with the "asset_events" name:
- If feasible, we should find a way to name things so that it's clear to users whether the dictionary includes keys for assets with no events
- Should it be `asset_materializations`?

I think the direction is good, that we are abstracting the way function get executed for actor reader/writer and normal functions. So should we call it StepExecutor and do this:

```
e = StepExecutor(step_type, None, None, catch_exceptions)

    from ray.experimental.workflow.common import WorkflowStatus
    for _ in range(step_max_retries):
        try:
            workflow_context.update_workflow_step_context(context, step_id)
            args, kwargs = _resolve_step_inputs(step_inputs)
            # in side run, it'll do:
            # wrap_run/commit/unpack
            ret = ret.run(func, *args, **kwargs)
            break
        except BaseException as e:
            error = e

    if step_type != StepType.READONLY_ACTOR_METHOD:
        if catch_exceptions or error is None
            _record_step_status(step_id, WorkflowStatus.FINISHED)
        else:
            _record_step_status(step_id, WorkflowStatus.RESUMABLE)
           raise error

    if catch_exceptions:
        return (error, ret)
    else:
        return ret
```

@ioanaif This is great, thank you! It looks like handling ListOffsetArray and IndexedOptionArray in `_getitem_next` is next.

I just made some tweaks and implemented enough for the skipped tests to pass. The skipped test "reasons" mentioned the lack of `toListOffsetArray`, but what was actually happening was that `Content.__getitem__` wasn't handling multi-dimensional NumPy arrays well: it was converting them into RegularArray of NumpyArray, but only NumpyArray was recognized as a rectilinear array type. If we let those RegularArrays be converted into ListOffsetArrays, they would do the slices incorrectly.

So now that's fixed and we _also_ have an implementation of `toListOffsetArray`.

Notice that `ListArray._broadcast_tooffsets64` and `ListOffsetArray._broadcast_tooffsets64` would be exactly the same, since the kernels only rely on the existence of `starts` and `stops`. (An `offsets` index can be zero-copy converted to `starts` and `stops`, but not the other way around.) Since this isn't C++, it's possible to implement it only on ListOffsetArray and have the ListArray class use ListOffsetArray's implementation, just as properties like `minmax_depth` reuse the Forms' implementations. This technique will likely help us minimize complexity in other methods, too.

I edited the text and structure of the forms; here's my mock-up: https://github.com/jpivarski/test-issue-syntax/issues/new/choose

In general, I'm minimizing the amount of explanatory text and reducing the structure to one textarea per form. The person filling out these forms is coming from some completely other task and having a lot of widgets on the screen is daunting.

If there's only one textarea, why use forms at all? Because the explanatory text can be outside the textarea, clearly separating the instructions we're giving them from the text they write. Even better: the instructions we're giving them don't disappear when they remove the original contents to start writing their text.

As a downside of the new forms, you can only drag & drop files into the textareas after you've clicked on them, so I've included a reminder in bold to click into the textarea first. It's also only possible to drop files or paste images if the textarea doesn't have a "render" field, so I've left that off.

It's cool that dedicated text fields for code to reproduce and log outputs will render the Python and tracebacks appropriately, but users are going to need more freedom to explain things, and one general-purpose textarea does that best. Some people are going to want to paste their error messages as screenshots because that's the only way they know how to do it. (I've included "Copy-pasted text is better than screenshots," but everyone is at a different skill level with respect to the terminal apps they're using.)

The ui urls and the service api urls are diverging a little bit, but they were already inconsistent. We don't have a formal interface around ui urls, which is something we may wish to address in future, so that people and systems can safely link to ui elements.

The queue.getArtifact URL path is:
`<ROOT_URL>/api/queue/v1/task/<TASK_ID>/runs/<RUN_ID>/artifacts/<ARTIFACT_PATH>`

The Task Inspector URL path for the live log artifact is now:
`<ROOT_URL>/tasks/<TASK_ID>/runs/<RUN_ID>/logs/live/<ARTIFACT_NAME>`

As you see, the path elements had already diverged (e.g. `/task` -> `/tasks`).

The setFocus should be -1 as described in the comment near line 130. The root cause is here:
https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/blob/master/frontend/src/components/worksheets/Worksheet/Worksheet.js#L666

The node here will be null (you can console.log the node) when we set the focus above the -1 item, since there is no item with id `codalab-worksheet-item--1`. A possible fix will to manually give the -1 item the id  codalab-worksheet-item--1 or some other special id, and find the item using that id when the passed in index argument is -1
Note that this was a partial dupe of #3544.

As for the deletion of `bin` and `*-info`, I would rather see it mimic `serverless-python-requirements` (partially to simplify moving between the two tools) like we did for the docker implementation. slim: true
```yaml
slimPatternsAppendDefaults: true
slimPatterns:
  - '**/*.egg-info*'
```

> For raw output, I agree with the decision to simply print the whole BUILD file. Way too tricky to otherwise try to identify which part is applicable. And we hope/expect BUILD files to be fairly small thanks to dep inference.

I played (a very small bit) with some inspection tools to find the applicable part, but it looked complicated. It could be an issue in its own right if we wanted to pursue it more.
 
> However, I could see it getting confusing when you specify multiple targets/files, and mapping what belongs to what. Thoughts on detecting the case of >1 "spec", and logging a warning to consider using the JSON format? We do that with `./pants py-constraints`:

I don't think a warning is necessary if we make JSON the default output, but I'll defer to your judgement on this.

> The JSON output is excellent: it shows the user what Pants's view of a particular file/target actually is. That transparency is great for debugging. We might want to make JSON the default?
> 
> I'd recommend two tweaks:
> 
>     1. Rather than `alias`, call it `target_type`, or something similar. "Alias" is an implementation detail.
> 
>     2. I think we want to render default values? That way, we show a complete view of what Pants is doing. It also allows for safer JSON queries, as you're guaranteed the key will exist. If it gets too chatty, we could consider an option like `--ignore-defaults`.
> 

I agree with these suggestions.

- [ ] Make JSON the default output style
- [ ] Rename `alias` in output to `target_type`
- [ ] Show default values by default
- [ ] Add `--exclude-defaults` flag (optional)

> In #4861, we mentioned adding a table format in a followup. I'm not sure that would really be necessary - the mix of Raw and JSON is very helpful already.

Agreed. It's human-readable enough, and machine readable with things like `jq`

> In #4861, I pondered
> 
> > Should [we] evaluate the sources and dependencies fields, or show the raw values?
> 
> After seeing this PR, I think no, we should not. `./pants dependencies` already exists for deps, and `./pants filedeps` for sources. It's redundant imo, and also non-trivial, to include those here, including a performance hit.

Agreed, and if you really want that, `jq` + `xargs` are your friends:

```
./pants peek ... | jq -r ... | xargs ./pants filedeps
```

Although I admit that trying to come up with a good example `jq` query made me realize that it doesn't seem to play well with arbitrary keys like we're using, so perhaps making a list of objects is a better way forward. Any opinion on this, @Eric-Arellano ?

Thanks! This is fantastic for isolating syncs from one another. I've never played with DB advisory locks, but they seem very powerful for this type of use case -- thanks for the TIL!

I made one note about selecting the partition to lock on, but a more fundamental thought: This looks like it should handle potential conflicts between multiple syncs, but the other side of this I forgot to mention but am remembering now is the need for snapshotting during serialization, where the app models may be getting modified in parallel through the app's API etc, while a serialization task is in progress. So without read-level isolation there, we may pull some records from app models and serialize them, then the app makes changes to both the models we already pulled and some we're about to pull, leading to inconsistencies in what we put into the Store. Is there a special transaction type we could use just for the serialization operation to ensure we're reading from a static snapshot over the course of that serialization, across all the tables we're looping over?

Related to that, and also happening during serialization, was the [incrementing of the current local instance counter](https://github.com/learningequality/morango/blob/7c497ec6401af50bb382902f068bd619af972b04/morango/models/core.py#L194), and ensuring it's an in-place increment rather than a "get and then set" operation that could have race conditions (and lead to two serializations using the same counter etc). I _think_ we may be OK with the current implementation, because it uses an `F` field reference for single-statement incrementing rather than a read/write, but if you think it would be safer, we could add an instance-level lock in there too.
I think this should probably be broken up into separate PRs, potentially one for core, one for graphql, one for JS.

Thanks, have a few comments:

The main place where paths are stored now is `StorageContext`. I've actually had a TODO about how this handles windows and relative paths, so would be a good time to fix it up.

StorageContext takes in the exact user `storage_path` that's passed, as well as an optional `storage_filesystem`. Here's what I was imagining the path handling could look like:

```python
class StorageContext:
    def __init__(self, storage_path: Tuple[str, os.PathLike], storage_filesystem: Optional[pyarrow.fs.FileSystem]):
        if storage_filesystem provided:
            # just convert storage_path to posix path (don't convert to an absolute path)
            # this is because the path should stay relative to the custom_fs
        elif local path (scheme == file or no scheme):
            # expand and convert to *absolute* posix path
        else: # do nothing for URIs, which will get handled by pyarrow.
            assert is_uri(storage_path)
```

Then, once we've converted it once in the constructor, we can keep using `os.path.join` using these paths elsewhere.

I wanted to avoid checking `is_local_path` in this class, but it seems like it's necessary to support relative / windows paths, since pyarrow will raise an error from the input to `pyarrow.fs.from_uri`.

This generally looks good, thanks! I haven't verified that it covers all `SDL_GetError()` calls, but it's definitely an improvement.

As a general comment, you've added a bunch of `SDL_ClearError()` calls that are harmless but probably unnecessary. If you are using the recommended pattern, like this pseudocode:

```
ret = SDL_Something()
if ret indicates failure:
    handle error SDL_GetError()
```

then there's no need to call `SDL_ClearError()` before `SDL_Something()`. The only time you really need `SDL_ClearError()` is when a function doesn't tell you whether it succeeded or not, like this pseudocode:

```
SDL_ClearError()
SDL_SomeFunctionReturningVoid()
error = SDL_GetError()
if error:
    warning "perhaps SDL_SomeFunctionReturningVoid() failed, or perhaps this is harmless, I can't really tell: {error}"
```

to minimize the chance that the error indicator is still set for some unrelated reason.

no, that's not how we implement this, it should be done by checking sanity of the extension, that the extracted list is not empty. The ceiling limit is implicit as the specified length of encoded length is one byte, and items are two bytes long.

**Design question:** At the API level, could we consolidate `prefetch_blocks` and `async_fetch_batch` with a single `prefetch_batches` argument that gives the number of batches to asynchronously prefetch?

## Motivation

I think this will be a lot cleaner from an end-user perspective, with a single argument controlling prefetching behavior that has clear batch-based semantics. We ultimately care about **pipelining upstream batch preparation with downstream batch computation**, and both prefetching batches over the network and background batch slicing/concatenation/formatting fall under the former, so having a single batch-based argument controlling how many batches we want to prepare while a single batch is being processed seems like better UX. IMO, the object store transfer prefetching should probably be an implementation detail in facilitating a generic "how many batches to we want to prepare in the background on this worker" API, similar to the semantics of the [`tf.data` prefetching API](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/data/Dataset#prefetch).

## Do We Need Two Knobs?

Since this seems like nicer UX, the only reasons that we'd want to have two knobs (that I can think of) are:
1. We want to have different levels of prefetching for object store transfers compared to background slicing/concatenation/format conversion.
2. The prefetching unit for the two args should be different (e.g. batch vs. block).

(1) could be true. If we assume that we're not hitting NIC bandwidth or CPU bottlenecks during deserialization or batch prep, then prefetching object store transfers is ultimately limited by the node's object store capacity, while background slicing/concatenation/format conversion is ultimately limited by the node's worker heap, which are separate resource pools with different capacities. This is further complicated by format conversions leading to memory inflation. E.g. for a Pandas batch format UDF on Arrow blocks executed on a node that has equal object store and worker heap allocations, you could imagine that we want to prefetch 8 batches to the object store, but only do 1 background batch slice and format conversion since the Pandas format conversion results in a DataFrame that's 4x larger than the Arrow data (and the UDF will need to at least allocate an output in the worker heap as well); this inflation could easily happen for boolean data, for example, which is bitpacked in Arrow but is not in Pandas.

However, this may be a non-issue if:
* the Python worker heap is typically the bottleneck rather than the node's object store (I think this is true for transformations/trainers that don't use a NumPy/Arrow narrow waist), or;
* we can rely on Ray Core's object pull manager to do proper backpressure on object transfers (I believe this is true).

The user can set `prefetch_batches` tailored to whatever eliminates compute resource stalls subject to a Python worker heap cap, and object transfer prefetching will do a best-effort fulfillment of `prefetch_batches`, subject to Ray Core's backpressure on object transfers when the node's object store pressure gets high. I'm thinking that this might make (1) a non-issue.

(2) is the status quo, but I think that the current object store prefetching API would be better expressed in terms of batches (this has been a long-standing TODO), so I don't think that (2) is a concern.

## Semantics and Implementation

For `.map_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics, except that the buffer size is configurable. Down the line, we might have this arg control the number of blocks that are prefetched in the actor compute model.

For `.iter_batches()`, this `prefetch_batches` arg would have the existing `async_fetch_batch` semantics along with exposing the buffer size, _and_ would control how many blocks are prefetched into the object store on the consumer node. Namely, if we know the number of rows in a block from the readily-available block metadata (this should be defined in the common case), we would prefetch `N` blocks such that `N` is the smallest number of blocks that satisfies `prefetch_batches`, i.e. we choose an `N` such that `sum([b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N]) >= prefetch_batches * batch_size` and `sum([b.num_rows for b in blocks[:N-1]) < prefetch_batches * batch_size`. If the block metadata doesn't have the number of rows in the block (e.g. the dataset is a lazy read of CSV files, for which we don't know the number of rows until the entire files has been read), then we can prefetch one block at a time.

Thanks for the PR @Charlottez112! I added some comments.

We supported "in place" operations in a previous version of freud, but I am hesitant to re-introduce in-place operations. I think parameters like this introduce API confusion and inconsistency with _every other method_ that could possibly operate in place but hasn't been enabled. It means we have to double (or at least augment) our testing code, worry about whether data is read-only (we just closed #424), keep more documentation up to date, etc. and the benefits are small (avoiding one extra copy of data). I'm open to others' thoughts on this but as a maintainer I am unsure about reneging on our changes in the freud v2 design.

Does this share some changes with #3419?

Something is rotten in the state of Denmark.

The behaviour used to be:
- for each input file representing a physical page (which could be PAGE-XML, a single image file or a single file of some other type),
  - if PAGE-XML, just update the pcGtsId and append to Metadata, writing a copy in the output fileGrp
  - otherwise, make a byte-by-byte copy in the output fileGrp; further, if it _was_ an image file, create a new PAGE-XML referencing that image file as original

We were dissatisfied with that, because copying images is generally not necessary and also not typical for OCR-D. (Images are always re-referenced by new PAGE-XML. Only new images must be added – if the processor operates on images. But ocrd-dummy is not such a processor. You can use ocrd-preprocess-image for that kind of thing.)

IMHO all that was necessary was to add a parameter `copy_files=false` and change the non-PAGE case such that the copying is dependent on that parameter (plus the image reference filename is, because you must use the new filename iff you created a new one).

However, the new behaviour here becomes:
- for each input file representing a physical page (which could be PAGE-XML, a single image file or a single file of some other type),
  - if PAGE-XML
    - **if `copy_files=true`**, then update the pcGtsId and append to Metadata, writing a copy in the output fileGrp
    - **if `copy_files=false`**, then do nothing (!)
  - otherwise,
    - **if `copy_files=true`**, then make a byte-by-byte copy in the output fileGrp
    - **if `copy_files=false`**, then do nothing
    - further, if it was an image file, create a new PAGE-XML referencing that image file as original (but always the _old_ image file!)

Why would you want to do this?

Excellent work! The changes on the R side look good to me, and I appreciate you making the interface there 1-based to match R's conventions. LightGBM's R package has historically been inconsistent about that (see, for example, discussion in #4970).

I left a few very minor suggestions for the R package.

----------------------

I also think there's one other thing we should talk about now, on this PR. I believe it'd be useful to have a similar interface, but where you could get the number of bins _by feature name_.

I think that exposing that would prevent the need for users to write code like this to achieve the same thing when using named data objects like data frames:

```python
feat_indx = [
    i, _
    for feat_name in df.columns
    if feat_name = "my_cool_feature"
][0]
ds.feature_num_bin(feat_indx)
```

I see three options for that:

1. Don't do this at all.
2. Allow the input to `feature_num_bin()` in the Python and R packages to accept `Union[int, str]`
    * assume that a string passed to this method should be matched exactly in `feature_names`
3. Keep `feature_num_bin()` in the Python and R packages as-is and later add a new `Dataset` method like `feature_num_bin_by_feature_name()`
    * this accepts a string, matches it to `feature_names` to get the index (if `feature_names` have been defined), then calls `feature_num_bin()` with that index.

I only bring this up here in this review because if we want to eventually do Option 2, then I'd recommend changing the argument name to `feature` instead of `feature_idx`.

I have a mild preference for Option 3, as I think having explicit methods without union types makes it easier to reason about how the code is going to work. But this project already uses union types in so many other places, I'm not opposed to Option 2.

What do you think? also tagging @StrikerRUS @shiyu1994 @guolinke for other opinions.

I've played with the example and looked through most of the code and I'm super impressed! Great job Lisa! I wouldn't change very much, just the default behaviour for old users and introduce a very conservative suggestion for the ``global_constraints``.

## Naming schemes

I agree with your naming: we need ``investment_periods`` to distinguish from ``snapshot_periods`` that run through the year. Old PyPSA "overnight" behaviour is often called "multi-period" optimisation, but we need to make clear that we also do multiple investment periods here.

## Default behaviour

The guiding principle is that old users shouldn't have to make any big changes, and that there should be no surprises.

I suggest that n.snapshots stays the same, and can be either MultiIndex or Index.

All PyPSA functions should by default treat MultiIndex snapshots just the same as Index snapshots. This is the case for old PyPSA ``n.lopf(pyomo=False)``, but ``n.lopf(pyomo=True)``, ``n.lpf()`` and ``n.pf()`` all break when using MultiIndex - we should fix this in future releases (but not now).

The multi-period optimisation is the only one to treat MultiIndex differently, and must be activated with:

``n.lopf(pyomo=False, multi_investment_periods=True)``

When this is activated, it checks that ``n.snapshots`` is MultiIndex, that level 0 is integer, and that the integers are increasing. It also checks that all investment periods appear in the index of ``n.investment_period_weightings`` and vice-versa, throwing an error if not.

If ``n.lopf(pyomo=True, multi_investment_periods=True)`` is called, an error is thrown. By default ``multi_investment_periods=False``.

### Snapshot weightings

This is a parallel change to the investment periods. In PyPSA Version 0.18.0 we will replace the pd.Series ``n.snapshot_weightings`` with a pd.DataFrame with three columns ``[objective_weightings, generator_weightings, store_weightings]``. This separates out the three current use cases of weightings in the LOPF:

- ``objective_weightings``: for operational costs in the objective function
- ``store_weightings``: for elapsed time in the filling of ``Store`` and ``StorageUnit`` components
- ``generator_weightings``: for the contribution of generators to ``GlobalConstraint`` components for e.g. CO2 budgets

Example use cases:

- 3-hourly sampling for full year: ``objective_weighting = generator_weighting = store_weighting = 3``.
- hourly sampling for 7 days to represent full year of 52 weeks with cyclic storage: ``objective weighting = generator_weighting = 52. , store_weighting = 1.``

BUT this case fails if e.g. a ``Store`` represents non-cyclic depletion of e.g. biomass or some other finite resource, in which case you want ``store_weighting = 52``, like ``generator_weighting``. This interface needs all storage doing short-term cycling, or all storage non-cyclic (depleting a finite resource). We should fix this when we implement Kotzur's seasonal storage scheme.

### Investment period weightings

I think the way Lisa implemented this looks good, maybe change the names of weightings to be similar to the ``snapshot_weightings``?

I also suggest not to have ``n.investment_periods``, but track these only via level 0 of ``n.snapshots``.

### Behaviour when an empty network is generated

If you call ``n = pypsa.Network()`` by default will be created:

- ``n.snapshots = pd.Index(["now"])``
- ``n.snapshot_weightings = pd.DataFrame([[1.,1.,1.]],index=["now"],
                                     columns=["objective_weightings","store_weightings","generator_weightings"])``
- ``n.investment_period_weightings = pd.DataFrame(columns=["objective_weightings","store_weightings","generator_weightings"])``

### Behaviour when a network from PyPSA Version < 0.18.0 is imported

Create new empty ``n.investment_period_weightings`` like for empty network above.

The values of pd.Series ``n.snapshot_weightings`` are copied across all three values of the new pd.DataFrame ``n.snapshot_weightings``.

## Global constraints for ``p_nom_max`` at each bus, carrier and investment_period

I suggest to keep these all in ``n.global_constraints`` since they are global in the sense of affecting multiple components at once. We need to add to the ``GlobalConstraint`` table attributes ``bus``, ``carrier`` and ``investment_period`` and type ``multi_investment_period_p_nom_max`` or similar.

Yes, this will create lots of global constraints, but introducing e.g. ``n.global_constraints_p`` doesn't help since you need 3-d information.

This way is minimally invasive.

## Treatment of sub_networks

I think the current way is good. The sub_networks are generated as needed, and the last one to be generated is stored. There is no need to store them from all investment periods, i.e. no need for ``n.sub_networks_p``.

The sub_network is anyway more of a hidden variable.

## Storage behaviour between investment periods

Did I understand correctly that cyclic storage is cyclic within each investment period? This behaviour would be fine.

If the storage is not cyclic, is energy transfered between investment periods? Probably this is not such a good idea for e.g. depletable biomass. But maybe we should model depletable biomass as a generator, rather than as a store that starts full. Then the generator can fill the storage at the start of each year?

## Check objective function

Since the objective function is quite complicated now, please check for some non-trivial examples that the Gurobi objective function is also what you calculated from the results. This should help to find bugs.

## Coding style

Try to avoid abbreviations like ``inv_p`` and ``sns`` in API functions like ``network.determine_network_topology``. Here we should be explicit for understandability. You can abbreviate inside functions, that's fine.

Also try to avoid committing code very specific to your own setup, like paths to libraries.

Actually, one more thing: some of these might not be active in any tests. You can find this out by putting "`raise Exception`" before every string, run the tests, see the failures, and remove each "`raise Exception`" that you see in the test failures until all the tests run fine but there are still some "`raise Exception`" lines left. Be extra-careful about those lines: those are the ones that could introduce an error that we don't see.

(This technique is a poor-man's coverage tool, but it's also a directed coverage tool: at the moment, you're only interested in the coverage of these string-generations.)

In this case the tarfile is produced by a local project a user just asked Pex to build. As such, that project is both implicitly trusted and, even if not, can do worse in the process of building the tar file. Depending on the build process for the local Python project, it could use setup.py and that can contain arbitrary code let alone arbitrary extraction paths in a tarfile built from it.

Both because of this and the fact this rips through the tarfile twice in order to sanity check the members, I'm disinclined to accept this patch unless you can point out something I'm missing here. I am not a security expert, but it does seem like building one's own code exempts itself from your concerns here.

I believe there is spaceto improve user experience further beyond adding parity with nbclassic:
1. It's not clear what exactly "Close and Halt" and "Shut Down" items do and what is the difference between them. Please find suggestions below on renaming "Close and Halt" into "Close Notebook and Shutdown Kernel". "Shut Down" should then be renamed into "Shut Down Server". I think this can be done within this PR but can also be taken care of separately.
2. "Close and Halt"  terminates kernel of the notebook and "Shut Down" terminates server with all kernels. When this happens all unsaved data is lost. Therefore these buttons are destructive and can lead to data loss. To remedy this, we could ask a user to confirm his action via modal/popup window. We should then also add "..." to the end of both options to indicate that selecting the item will lead to further options or require additional input. It would probably be best to do it in a separate PR after Notebook 7 launch, please let me know if you agree with the approach and I can create an issue to track it.

Even if you do punt on handling ambiguity, the code maintainability might benefit from more closely following the structure of `pants/backend/python/dependency_inference`:

- two rules to create `GoFirstPartyImportPathMapping` and `GoThirdPartyImportPathMapping`
- one rule to go from a single `GoImportPath` to `GoImportPathOwner`, which has an `owner: Address` and (eventually) `ambiguous: tuple[Address, ...]`.
- this rule you have now to use the dependency inference plugin hook

This delineation has been really helpful in Python land for having more focused tests.
